title: Amino acid biosynthetic cost anti-correlates with expression in E. coli (Δ=0.60 ATP/residue, 2.5%) and B. subtilis (Δ=0.40, 1.8%) — the cross-species validation of the cost-expression gradient

subject.display: Migration cohort for 'Amino acid biosynthetic cost anti-correlates with expression in E. coli (Δ=0.60 '
subject.note: Inclusion predicates intentionally empty at migration time; the implicit member set is defined by the v1.1 premises. Re-author with explicit inclusion predicates to tighten the subject.

source.note: Mean ATP/residue costs by expression quintile from Akashi & Gojobori 2002 Table 1. Covers E. coli and B. subtilis protein-coding genes. Staged for MOD-3 from their published Supplementary data.

premises.note: 10 rows: 2 species × 5 expression quintiles. Q1 is lowest expression, Q5 is highest.

operations.note: Per-species extraction of the Q1 (max-cost) and Q5 (min-cost) mean values so their difference quantifies the monotonic gradient slope.
MOD-3 effect-size table: (Q1 − Q5) in absolute ATP/residue and as % of Q1.

statistics.name: ecoli_q1_minus_q5_atp_per_residue
statistics.note: E. coli K12. From AG2002 Table 1 quintile means: Q1=23.50, Q5=22.90.

statistics.name: bsubtilis_q1_minus_q5_atp_per_residue
statistics.note: B. subtilis 168. From AG2002 Table 1: Q1=23.20, Q5=22.80.

statistics.name: quintile_mean_atp_per_residue_by_species
statistics.note: The four load-bearing quintile means as a LabeledValue[] vector.

inference.justification: Three conjuncts license the cross-species validation: (1)+(2) both E. coli and B. subtilis show positive Q1−Q5 cost gradients — the cost-expression anti-correlation holds in both prokaryotes; (3) E. coli's effect size exceeds B. subtilis's — consistent with E. coli's larger effective population size (Ne_Ec ≈ 10^8 vs Ne_Bs ≈ 10^7) and therefore stronger cost-selection regime. This conjunct is stat-vs-stat, exercising the cross-effect-size comparison shape.
inference.failure_mode: If either species's gradient reversed (Q5 > Q1) the cross-species universality claim collapses. If B. subtilis's gradient exceeded E. coli's, the Ne-scaling prediction direction fails and the selection-regime framing would need revising.

conclusion.assertion: In E. coli K12 and B. subtilis 168, mean amino acid biosynthetic cost declines monotonically with expression quintile — E. coli by 0.60 ATP/residue (2.5% of Q1), B. subtilis by 0.40 ATP/residue (1.8%). The cross-species universality and the E. coli > B. subtilis effect-size ordering are consistent with cost-optimization under population-size-scaled selection.

scope.scale_note: Effect sizes are per-species quintile means, not per-gene correlations. The per-gene Spearman ρ is tabulated in AG2002 but not pinned here.

confidence.note: Per-species significance is in AG2002 Table 1; this fixture pins the effect-size context for MOD-3 rather than the significance test.

external_assumptions.statement: Akashi & Gojobori 2002 Table 1 is a faithful summary of per-residue cost by expression quintile in the two species; staging their values as Q1..Q5 means from their Supplementary data is accurate to the nearest 0.1 ATP/residue.
Prokaryotic quintile means are comparable to the human per-gene Spearman approach modulo units — both are sensitive to the same cost-expression gradient.
